title: Privilege Escalation — sudo Misconfiguration (find -exec)
description: Abuse a careless passwordless sudo rule that permits the webadmin account to run /usr/bin/find as root. Use find's -exec flag to execute arbitrary commands as root and read a flag accessible only by root.
outcomes: SSH into a low-privilege webadmin account and read the onboarding note.
Enumerate sudo permissions with sudo -l and identify the allowed command.
Understand why find's -exec flag turns any root-level find invocation into arbitrary root command execution.
Use sudo find -exec to read a root-owned flag file that is otherwise inaccessible.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Work through the levels in order — each level leads naturally into the next.
* Use hints when stuck. Hints cost points but less than giving up.
* Only attack systems in your sandbox.

# Storyline

You have obtained low-privilege credentials for **webadmin** on a **Meridian Systems** application server. The credentials were recovered from a misconfigured deployment script left in a public repository — a common mistake in DevOps pipelines. Your task is to escalate those credentials from low-privilege access to full root control.

The Meridian DevOps team wanted `webadmin` to be able to clean up old Apache log files without needing to call a senior administrator. Their solution: grant `webadmin` passwordless sudo access to `/usr/bin/find`, reasoning that find is a harmless read-only utility used only for locating files.

This reasoning is fatally wrong.

`find` is a GTFO-able binary — it appears on GTFOBins (https://gtfobins.github.io), a curated list of Unix binaries that can be abused for privilege escalation. The reason: find's `-exec` flag executes an arbitrary shell command for every file it matches. When `find` runs as root, every `-exec` command also runs as root. A single `sudo find / -exec /bin/sh -i \;` gives you a root shell.

Passwordless sudo rules that target powerful utilities are a chronic misconfiguration in Linux system administration. The principle of least privilege demands that each account have only the permissions it strictly needs — and find has far more capability than 'list files for cleanup'.

In this exercise, you will authenticate as `webadmin`, discover the sudo rule, understand why it is dangerous, and exploit it to read a flag in `/root/flag.txt` — a file readable only by root.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.34.23**, then authenticate as webadmin on the target:

```
ssh webadmin@10.1.34.10
Password: Deploy2024!
```

The target server is at **10.1.34.10** (SSH port 22). Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
Authenticate as webadmin on the local target:

```
ssh webadmin@127.0.0.1 -p 2222
Password: Deploy2024!
```

Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Identify the SSH Service Port
levels.content:
Begin with a port scan to confirm the SSH service is running and identify its port:

```
nmap -sV -p 1-1024 10.1.34.10
```

Look for the port with `ssh` in the SERVICE column. Note the version string — it tells you the SSH implementation and version, which is useful for determining whether the version has any known authentication vulnerabilities.

In this scenario the SSH service is on the standard port. The credentials you already have (`webadmin` / `Deploy2024!`) give you an initial foothold — the question is what you can do from there.

**What port is the SSH service listening on?**
levels.solution:
```
$ nmap -sV -p 1-1024 10.1.34.10
PORT   STATE SERVICE VERSION
22/tcp open  ssh     OpenSSH 8.9p1 Ubuntu ...
```

SSH is on port **22**.

hints.title: How to scan
hints.content: Run: nmap -sV -p 1-1024 10.1.34.10. Look for the line where SERVICE says 'ssh'. The port number is on the left.

hints.title: The SSH port
hints.content: SSH runs on port 22 by default. The nmap output shows: 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH...

levels.title: Check sudo Permissions
levels.content:
You are logged in as `webadmin`. The first step in local privilege escalation is always to enumerate what elevated permissions the account already has.

After SSH-ing into the target:

```
ssh webadmin@10.1.34.10
```

Enter password: `Deploy2024!`

Once logged in, list the sudo rules that apply to this account:

```
sudo -l
```

Also read the onboarding note:

```
cat ~/onboarding.txt
```

The note hints at what the sudo access is intended for. Your goal is to determine whether the intended use can be abused.

**What command do you run to list the current user's sudo privileges?** (two words with a space)
levels.solution:
```
$ sudo -l
Matching Defaults entries for webadmin on target:
    env_reset, mail_badpass

User webadmin may run the following commands on target:
    (root) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/find
```

The command is **sudo -l**.

hints.title: How to enumerate sudo rules
hints.content: After logging in, run: sudo -l. This lists every command the current user may run with elevated privileges, including whether a password is required.

hints.title: The command
hints.content: The command is: sudo -l. (The -l flag means 'list'.)

hints.title: The answer
hints.content: The command to list sudo privileges is: sudo -l

levels.title: Identify the Allowed Command
levels.content:
Read the `sudo -l` output carefully. It will show a line like:

```
(root) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/find
```

This means `webadmin` may run `/usr/bin/find` as root with no password required. The `NOPASSWD:` qualifier is significant — there is no interactive password prompt to slow down automated exploitation.

Search GTFOBins in your mind: `find` is listed there. The reason is its `-exec` flag, which executes an arbitrary shell command for every matched file. When `find` runs as root, `-exec` commands also run as root.

Read the onboarding.txt note:

```
cat ~/onboarding.txt
```

It confirms this was intended as a log-cleanup helper. The DevOps team did not realise they were granting root command execution to any user who can log in as webadmin.

**What command (basename only, not the full path) is webadmin allowed to run as root?**

hints.title: How to read the sudo rule
hints.content: The sudo -l output shows the full path: /usr/bin/find. The command basename (the part after the last /) is the answer.

hints.title: What the answer is
hints.content: The command is: find. It is in /usr/bin/find — the DevOps team granted passwordless sudo access to the find utility.

levels.title: Understand find -exec
levels.content:
The key to exploiting this misconfiguration is `find`'s command execution flag.

`find` can run an arbitrary command for every file it matches, using this syntax:

```
find PATH -name PATTERN -exec COMMAND \;
```

For example, to run `id` as root:

```
sudo find / -name flag.txt -exec id \;
```

Because `sudo find` runs `find` as root, every `-exec` command is also spawned as root. There is no restriction on what command you can pass to `-exec` — it can be `cat`, `id`, `cp`, `/bin/bash`, or anything else on the filesystem.

This flag is so dangerous that GTFOBins documents it as a standard privilege escalation path for any context where find runs with elevated privileges.

**What flag does find use to execute an arbitrary command for each matched file?** (include the leading dash)
levels.solution: The flag is **-exec**. Full usage: `find PATH -name PATTERN -exec COMMAND \;`. The `\;` terminates the command list passed to -exec.

hints.title: How find executes commands
hints.content: find has a flag that runs an arbitrary command for each matched file. The syntax is: find PATH -exec COMMAND \;. The flag starts with a dash.

hints.title: The flag name
hints.content: The flag is -exec. With sudo find running as root, -exec gives you root-level command execution for any command you choose.

levels.title: Retrieve the Root Flag
levels.content:
Combine what you know: `webadmin` can run `sudo find` as root, and `find` can execute arbitrary commands with `-exec`. Use this to read `/root/flag.txt` — a file with mode `0400` readable only by root:

```
sudo find /root -name flag.txt -exec cat {} \;
```

Breaking down the command:
- `sudo find` — run find as root
- `/root` — search in root's home directory
- `-name flag.txt` — match the file
- `-exec cat {} \;` — for each match, run `cat` on it (`{}` is replaced by the matched file path)

The flag will be printed to your terminal. Without the sudo rule, `cat /root/flag.txt` would return 'Permission denied' for any non-root user.

**Submit the flag from /root/flag.txt.**

hints.title: How to read a root-only file with find -exec
hints.content: Run: sudo find /root -name flag.txt -exec cat {} \;. The sudo makes find run as root. The -exec cat {} \; reads each matched file. The {} is replaced by the file path.

hints.title: Why {} and \; are needed
hints.content: {} is a placeholder that find replaces with the matched file path. \; ends the -exec command. Without the backslash, the shell would interpret the semicolon as a command separator.

hints.title: Flag format
hints.content: The flag starts with WMG{ and ends with }. It is in /root/flag.txt, readable only via sudo find -exec.

levels.title: Read the Onboarding Note
levels.content:
Read the onboarding note in the webadmin home directory:

```
cat ~/onboarding.txt
```

The note explains why the sudo rule was created — it was signed off by a specific team who believed it was safe. In a real post-incident review, this note would be the starting point for identifying who authorised the misconfigured rule and why.

Note who signed the onboarding document. In a real engagement this detail goes in the finding report: 'The rule was authorised by the X team on date Y' — tracing the business context of the vulnerability.

**What team signed off the onboarding note?** (one word)
levels.solution:
```
$ cat ~/onboarding.txt
Meridian Systems — Web Ops Onboarding
For log cleanup you have been granted a passwordless sudo helper.
Run `sudo -l` to see what you are allowed to run. -- DevOps
```

The note was signed by **DevOps**.

hints.title: How to read the note
hints.content: Run: cat ~/onboarding.txt. The note is in the webadmin home directory and explains the purpose of the sudo rule.

hints.title: What to look for
hints.content: The note ends with a signature. Look at the last line — the signature is prefixed with --.

hints.title: The signing team
hints.content: The note is signed: -- DevOps. The team name is DevOps.

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve this game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, had you exploited a sudo misconfiguration?

choices.text: Yes, I had done this before
I knew about GTFOBins but had not used it
No, this was completely new to me

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What is the correct fix for this sudo misconfiguration?

choices.text: Remove the sudo rule and use a restricted helper script instead
Require a password before running sudo find
Allow only specific find arguments in the sudoers rule
Restrict webadmin to running find only in /var/log

questions.text: What would you change or add to improve this game?
